questions.question: Explain the black box problem in AI and why it is a concern for AI deployment.
keyPoints: Black box refers to opacity of complex models like deep neural networks
Concerns include lack of trust, inability to debug, regulatory compliance issues
Makes it difficult to identify bias or errors in decision-making

questions.question: Differentiate between LIME and SHAP as Explainable AI techniques.
keyPoints: LIME uses local linear approximations around specific predictions
SHAP uses Shapley values from game theory for feature attribution
Both are model-agnostic but differ in theoretical foundations and consistency

questions.question: List and briefly explain three types of bias that can occur in AI systems.
keyPoints: Training data bias - historical discrimination in data
Selection bias - non-representative sampling
Confirmation bias - reinforcing existing beliefs through personalization

questions.question: What is algorithmic accountability and why is it important in AI deployment?
keyPoints: Organizations must be answerable for AI outcomes
Requires documentation, audit trails, and mechanisms for redress
Essential for building trust and meeting regulatory requirements

questions.question: Explain the concept of counterfactual explanations in Explainable AI.
keyPoints: Describes what changes would alter the prediction
Answers 'what if' questions for users
Helps understand model behavior through hypothetical scenarios

questions.question: Define model-agnostic interpretability and give two examples of such techniques.
keyPoints: Methods applicable to any ML model without internal access
LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations)
SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)

questions.question: Discuss the relationship between privacy and AI ethics.
keyPoints: AI systems often require large amounts of personal data
Data minimization principle reduces privacy risks
GDPR and similar regulations protect individuals from invasive AI profiling

questions.question: Explain the key principles of AI ethics and discuss how they can be implemented in practice.
keyPoints: Five core principles: Fairness, Transparency, Accountability, Privacy, Human-centered design
Fairness requires bias detection and mitigation techniques
Transparency involves documenting AI systems and providing explanations
Accountability requires governance frameworks and audit mechanisms
Privacy needs data minimization and anonymization techniques
Implementation requires technical solutions, policies, and continuous monitoring

questions.question: Compare intrinsic interpretability with post-hoc interpretability. Provide examples of techniques used in each category.
keyPoints: Intrinsic: interpretable by design (decision trees, linear models, rule-based systems)
Post-hoc: explanations generated after training (LIME, SHAP, feature importance)
Trade-offs between model complexity and interpretability
Choice depends on use case requirements and regulatory needs

questions.question: Discuss the challenges in achieving AI fairness. How can different fairness metrics lead to conflicting outcomes?
keyPoints: Fairness metrics can be mathematically incompatible (Impossibility Theorems)
Equalized odds vs predictive parity vs demographic parity conflicts
Trade-off between fairness and accuracy
Different contexts require different fairness definitions
Solutions require careful consideration of social context and stakeholder input

questions.question: Explain the concept of adversarial machine learning and its implications for AI security and ethics.
keyPoints: Adversarial ML exploits vulnerabilities through carefully crafted inputs
Can cause AI systems to make catastrophic errors
Implications: security risks in autonomous systems, need for robust models
Ethical concerns: malicious use, attack surfaces in AI deployment
Defense mechanisms: adversarial training, input validation
